---
title: "Spring 2026 Exam 3"
subtitle: "SDS 164 Data Science 1"
format: 
  pdf: 
    fontsize: "12pt" 
    extra_dependencies: float 
    include-in-header:
      - text: |
          \usepackage{scrlayer-scrpage}
          \usepackage{ucharcat}
          \usepackage{float}
          \floatplacement{table}{H}
          \usepackage{lipsum} 
          \usepackage{subcaption}
          \renewcommand{\thesubfigure}{}
          \renewcommand{\thesubtable}{}
          % Optional: remove spaces/parentheses
          \captionsetup[table]{labelformat=empty}
          \captionsetup[subtable]{labelformat=empty}
          \clearpairofpagestyles
          \ofoot*{\pagemark}
          \cfoot{}
editor: source
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

\vspace{-.5cm}

*I pledge my honor that on this examination I have neither given nor received assistance not explicitly approved by the professor and that I have seen dishonest work.*

\vspace{1cm}

Name: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\

\vspace{1cm}

Signature: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_ Date: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

\vspace{1cm}

I have intentionally not signed the pledge \_\_\_\_ (check box if appropriate)

```{r}
#| label: Setup
#| include: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false

knitr::opts_chunk$set(fig.pos = "H", out.extra = "")
library(tidyverse)
library(gapminder)
library(ggthemes)
library(ggridges)
library(ggmosaic)
library(forcats)
library(lubridate)
library(viridis)
library(DT)
library(unvotes)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)

# Exam 3 is worth 50 points
# ~15 questions 


```

```{r}
#| label: Data
#| include: false
#| warning: false
#| message: false
data1 <- data.frame(c1 = 1:5,
                    c2 = 1:5,
                    c3 = 1:5,
                    c4 = 1:5,
                    c5 = 1:5)

stock_prices <- read_csv("~/Sds_164_S26/Class/Data/stock_prices.csv")
example_books <- read_csv("~/Sds_164_S26/Class/Data/example_books.csv")

q_fifteen <- list(
  tibble(
    Time = c("4 min", "3 min", "1 min")
    ),
  tibble(
    p.value = c(1e-16, 2e-4, 5e-1)
    ),
  tibble(
    Message = c("Ten Four","Negative","Watch your six")
    )
)

exam <- data.frame(
  Student = c(rep("Student A", 3),
              rep("Student B", 3),
              rep("Student C", 3)),
  Question = c(rep(paste("Question", 1:3))),
  Response = rbinom(9, size = 1, p = .5)) |> 
  mutate(Response = case_when(Response == 1 ~ "correct",
                              .default = "incorrect"))

df_x <- data.frame(id = c(1:3),
                    x = c("X1", "X2", "X3")) 

df_y <- data.frame(id = c(1,2,2,4),
                     y = c("Y1", "Y2", "Y2", "Y4"))

df_z <- data.frame(id = c(1,3,3),
                   x = c("X1", "X3", "X3"),
                   z = c("Z1", "Z3", "Z3"))

tbl_left <- left_join(df_x, df_y, join_by(id))
tbl_right <- right_join(df_y, df_z, join_by(id))

tbl_full <- full_join(df_x, df_y, join_by(id))

tbl_semi <- semi_join(df_x, df_z, join_by(id))
```

\newpage

**Refer to the chunk of code below for Questions 1 and 2.**

```{r}
#| label: Problem 1
#| eval: false
my_data   data.frame(x = "001", y = 1:3, z = c("A","B","C")) # <1>
write_csv(       , file = "~/Sds_164_S26/Data/data_name.csv") # <2> 
my_data |> pivot_wider(names_from = y, values_from = y) # <3> 
read_csv(my_data) # <4>
```

###### Question 1) Identify and correct three mistakes in the code above. \[6pts\]

\vspace{1.75cm}

###### Question 2) Match the line number from the code above to the appropriate steps. \[4pts\]

+--------------------------------------+-------------+
| Step                                 | Line Number |
+======================================+:===========:+
| 1.  Create a data frame.             |             |
+--------------------------------------+-------------+
| 2.  Save the data.                   |             |
+--------------------------------------+-------------+
| 3.  Read the data in to R.           |             |
+--------------------------------------+-------------+
| 4.  Pivot the data into wide format. |             |
+--------------------------------------+-------------+

\newpage

###### Question 3) Refer to the warning message below for the following questions. \[4pts\]

-   Why did we receiving this warning message?

\vspace{3cm}

-   What does this warning message tell us about the results from `parse_number`?

\vspace{3cm}



```{r}
#| label: Question 3
#| eval: true
#| warning: true
#| echo: false
#| attr-source: '.numberLines'

example_books |> 
  slice_tail(n = 3) |> 
  mutate(likes = parse_number(likes))

```

\newpage

###### Question 4) Refer to the code chunk below to answer the following questions \[6pts\]:

```{r}
#| label: Question 4
#| eval: false
example <- function(x, y){
  z <- x / sqrt(y)
  return(z)
}
example(x, y)
```

-   What inputs are needed for the function to run without producing an error message?

\vspace{4cm}

-   How would you describe the process that the function is written to do?

\vspace{4cm}

-   Would you expect the function to output a vector, data frame, or single value?

\vspace{3cm}

\newpage

###### Question 5) Complete the code to pivot the tibble into a wide format, then draw what the resulting wide data would look like. \[6pts\]

*Hint*: Assume that each observation should be a student.

::: {.column layout-ncol="2"}
```{r}
#| label: Question 5
#| echo: FALSE
print(as_tibble(exam))
```

<div>

```         
exam |>
  pivot_wider(
  
      names_from = 
      
      values_from =
      
      )
      
      
      
    
```

</div>
:::

\vspace{1.75cm}

\newpage

##### Directions: For Questions 6 to 10 refer to the tables below. The top 3 tables are unjoined datasets. The bottom 4 tables contain the results from joining 2 datasets (e.g., "Joined Data E" is the result of the anti_join of X and Y).

::: {#tbl-panel1 layout-ncol="3" layout-nrow="1"}
|  id | X   |
|----:|:----|
|   1 | X1  |
|   2 | X2  |
|   3 | X3  |

: Dataset X {#tbl-X}

|  id | Y   |
|----:|:----|
|   1 | Y1  |
|   2 | Y2  |
|   2 | Y2  |
|   4 | Y4  |

: Dataset Y {#tbl-Y}

|  id | X   | Z   |
|----:|:----|-----|
|   1 | X1  | Z1  |
|   3 | X3  | Z3  |
|   3 | X3  | Z3  |

: Dataset Z {#tbl-Z}

Unjoined Datasets
:::

::: {#tbl-panel2 layout-ncol="2" layout-nrow="2"}
|  id | X   | Y    |
|----:|:----|:-----|
|   1 | X1  | Y1   |
|   2 | X2  | Y2   |
|   2 | X2  | Y3   |
|   3 | X3  | `NA` |

: Joined Data A {#tbl-A}

|  id | X    | Y    |
|----:|:-----|:-----|
|   1 | X1   | Y1   |
|   2 | X2   | Y2   |
|   2 | X2   | Y3   |
|   3 | X3   | `NA` |
|   4 | `NA` | Y4   |

: Joined Data B {#tbl-B}

|  id | Y    | X   | Z   |
|----:|:-----|:----|:----|
|   1 | Y1   | X1  | Z1  |
|   3 | `NA` | X3  | Z3  |
|   3 | `NA` | X3  | Z3  |

: Joined Data C {#tbl-C}

|  id | X   |
|----:|:----|
|   1 | X1  |
|   3 | X3  |

: Joined Data D {#tbl-D}

Joined Datasets
:::

###### Question 6) What is a 'key' used for when joining data? \[3pts\]

*Provide a brief response, two to three sentences, using the tables above as a reference.*

\newpage

###### Question 7) Joined Data D is the result of a `semi_join` between which two datasets? \[4pts\]

\vspace{4cm}

###### Question 8) What are two aspects of Joined Data C that indicate it is the result of a `right_join` between Dataset Y and Dataset Z? \[4pts\]

\vspace{4cm}

###### Question 9) Which Joined Datasets are examples `mutating_joins` and which are examples of `filtering_joins`? \[4pts\]

\vspace{4cm}

###### Question 10) Why does Joined Data B have missing values for id = 4 in the X column and id = 3 in the Y column? \[4pts\]

\vspace{4cm}

\newpage

###### Question 11) Which of the following is the best reason for using long formatted data? \[1pt\]

a.  Having more observations and fewer variables is better.
b.  Longer data are easier for humans to read than wider data.
c.  It is easier to visualize and analyze long format data.

###### Question 12) Which of the following best describes one-to-one relationships? \[1pt\]

a.  One row in X matches to one row in Y.
b.  One row in X matches to many rows in Y.
c.  One row in X increases the rows in Y.

###### Question 13) A `filtering_join` such as `anti_join` or `semi_join` is likely to have what effect when joining data? \[1pt\]

a.  Filtering joins may remove everything but the unique values in a column.
b.  Filtering joins may increase the number of columns when joining data.
c.  Filtering joins may reduce the number of rows when joining data.

###### Question 14) Which of the following statements best describes tidy data? \[1pt\]

a.  Data that are tidy should be able to pick up after themselves and minimize their residuals without a trace.
b.  Data that are tidy have variables in their own columns, observations in their own rows and a single value in each cell.
c.  Data that are tidy should should only be in the long format so as to avoid being redundant.

###### Question 15) Which example best fits the need to use a function like `parse_number`? \[1pt\]

\vspace{.5cm}

::: {.column layout-ncol="3"}
```         
a. 
  Time 
  <chr>
1 4 min 
2 3 min 
3 1 min 
```

```         
b.
  p.value
    <dbl>
1   1e-16
2   2e- 4
3   5e- 1
```

```         
c.
  Message       
  <chr>         
1 Ten Four      
2 Negative      
3 Watch your six
```
:::

\newpage

## Extra Credit

Use the `rename()` function to give this penguin a new name and then use `ymd()` to give them a birthdate. Feel free to give the penguin some color, mutate the penguin a new hat, or a friend. \[+1pt\]

```{r}
#| eval: false
penguin |> 
  rename("                           " = NULL) |> 
  mutate(birthdate = ymd(                   ))
```

![](penguin.png){height="50%" fig-align="center"}
